key_insight: In multiobjective topology optimization, the Pareto front can be made of disconnected, nonconvex pieces from local fronts, and Pascoletti-Serafini scalarization gives a more uniform approximation than weighted sums or ε-constraint methods.
engineering_relevance: This helps engineers generate more reliable trade-off designs when balancing objectives such as compliance, volume, stress, and dynamic compliance in structural optimization.
method: Compared weighted-sum, ε-constraint, and Pascoletti-Serafini scalarization methods theoretically.
Showed that topology optimization Pareto fronts may be disconnected and locally structured, unlike the smooth fronts often assumed.
Performed numerical bi-objective studies across multiple structural objectives to assess front coverage and clustering behavior.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a broad numerical comparison on many bi-objective examples, with Pascoletti-Serafini producing more uniform front approximations and weighted sums showing clustering.
watch_item: Scalarization results can miss parts of the true Pareto set, so front coverage and local optimality should be checked carefully before selecting a design.
paper_title: Novel insights into Pareto fronts in multiobjective topology optimization and a comparative study of scalarization strategies
